mnemonics: CLOSE FAMILY: Cosine, Jaccard, and Euclidean are all Different Families of Proximity Measures used to see how CLOSE data points are.
keyPoints: Proximity measures quantify how similar or dissimilar data points are.
Different measures (e.g., Euclidean, Manhattan, Cosine, Jaccard) are suited for different data types and problems.
The choice of measure can drastically affect the results of clustering and classification algorithms.
commonMistakes: Using Euclidean distance for high-dimensional data without considering the 'curse of dimensionality'.
Applying a measure designed for continuous data (e.g., Euclidean) to categorical or binary data.
Forgetting to normalize features when using distance-based measures, giving undue weight to features on larger scales.
